name: Catharism
description: Catharism, also known as Albigensianism, was a Christian dualist or Gnostic movement that thrived in Southern France, particularly in the Languedoc region, during the 12th and 13th centuries. Cathars believed in two gods: a good god who created the spiritual realm and an evil god (often identified with the God of the Old Testament) who created the material world. They viewed the material world as inherently evil and sought to escape it through asceticism and a ritual of spiritual baptism called the Consolamentum. Proponents claim Catharism represented a purer, more authentic form of Christianity, free from the corruption of the Catholic Church. They point to biblical interpretations, the simplicity of Cathar rituals, and alleged Cathar virtues like pacifism and vegetarianism as evidence. Some alternative history narratives link Catharism to ancient Gnostic traditions and suggest they possessed secret knowledge or were guardians of the Holy Grail. Mainstream scholarship views Catharism as a heresy that arose from a confluence of social, economic, and religious factors. Historians emphasize the influence of earlier dualist movements from the East and the socio-political context of the Languedoc, where resentment against the Catholic Church was widespread. The Albigensian Crusade (1209-1229), launched by Pope Innocent III, aimed to eradicate Catharism through military force and resulted in widespread violence and the eventual suppression of the movement. While some Cathar beliefs might resonate with Gnostic ideas, there's no definitive proof of a direct lineage or that they held any 'secret knowledge' beyond their religious doctrines. Modern interpretations sometimes romanticize Catharism as a symbol of resistance against religious oppression, but it's crucial to acknowledge the complexities of their theology and the historical context of their persecution.
subtitle: Medieval dualist heresy in Southern France

claims.claim: The material world is evil and created by a false god.
claims.evidence: Biblical interpretations emphasizing the wickedness of the Old Testament God and the suffering inherent in the material world.
claims.criticism: Mainstream Christianity views the material world as created by God and inherently good, though corrupted by sin. The Old Testament God is seen as the same God as in the New Testament.

claims.claim: Catharism represents a purer, more authentic form of Christianity.
claims.evidence: The simplicity of Cathar rituals, their rejection of material wealth, and their emphasis on spiritual salvation.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians and theologians argue that Catharism's dualistic theology is fundamentally incompatible with core Christian doctrines, such as the Incarnation and the goodness of creation.

claims.claim: Cathars possessed secret knowledge or were guardians of the Holy Grail.
claims.evidence: Legends and speculative interpretations of historical events, often linking Catharism to earlier Gnostic traditions and the Knights Templar.
claims.criticism: There is no concrete historical evidence to support these claims. These narratives are largely based on romanticized and unsubstantiated theories.

keyFigures.name: Bernard Gui
keyFigures.role: Dominican Inquisitor, prominent persecutor of Cathars

keyFigures.name: Pierre Autier
keyFigures.role: Apostle of Catharism in the late 13th century

keyFigures.name: Walter L. Wakefield
keyFigures.role: Historian specializing in medieval heresies, including Catharism

timeline.event: Catharism begins to spread in Southern France.
The Albigensian Crusade, a military campaign to eradicate Catharism.
The last known Cathar Perfect, Guillaume Bélibaste, is burned at the stake.

sources.author: Walter L. Wakefield

sources.title: Walter L. Wakefield
